title: Performance, Migration, API Design, and Release Evidence · Diagnostic Repair Bay
summary: Migrate and publish a production-shaped TypeScript 7 library with measurable compiler performance and consumer proof. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a TypeScript 7 migration and release portfolio and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence d…
objectives: Measure cold and warm checks, project graphs, declarations, memory, and parallelism before changing configuration.
Migrate JavaScript incrementally with checkJs, JSDoc, allowJs, boundary validation, and shrinking suppression budgets.
Remove TypeScript 6 deprecations, compare TS6 and TS7 diagnostics, and document temporary API-tool compatibility.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Order the regression proof
steps.instruction: Order the Release Evidence evidence cycle for this debug task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Release Evidence assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: A debug build checks a two-package TypeScript 7 workspace with strict mode, ESM output, and one TypeScript 6 API-dependent tool. Record the emitted JavaScript, diagnostic code, runtime result, and the compatibility boundary before release 3.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Release Evidence has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Release Evidence precondition or invariant that the debug case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A debug build checks a two-package TypeScript 7 workspace with strict mode, ESM output, and one TypeScript 6 API-dependent tool. Record the emitted JavaScript, diagnostic code, runtime result, and the compatibility boundary before release 3.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Publishing successfully proves consumers can load and use the package.
